text: The XI robotic system is used to perform a left indirect inguinal hernia repair. The patient's full bladder is visible, and the indirect inguinal hernia can be observed.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision approximately seven to eight centimeters above the upper border of the internal ring, at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS). The incision is made in the preperitoneal space, between layers of the transversalis fascia. The surgeon proceeds medially, opting to stay in the preperitoneal space, and begins dissection in the lateral space, dividing multiple layers to reach the peritoneum.

The surgeon navigates medially, staying in the preperitoneal space, revealing the transversalis fascia adjacent to the rectus muscle and the visible inferior epigastric vessels. Observing the thin peritoneum, the surgeon transitions to the retro-rectus space medially, while remaining in the pre-peritoneal space laterally. A bridge of transversalis fascia is visible between these spaces, which the surgeon plans to cut through to connect them.

The surgeon navigates the plane with abundant areolar tissue adjacent to the bladder, visible on the right side of the screen. This maneuver occurs in the space of Retzius, allowing the bladder to be retracted downward while the retrorectus fat is brought down. The lobular retrorectus fat is moved to expose Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon follows the areolar tissue to establish a dissection plane for the inguinal hernia repair, minimizing bleeding. Due to previous prostate radiation, the surgeon carefully navigates around Cooper's ligament, noting visible edema in the area.

The surgeon uses scissors to dissect with short bursts of energy, aiming to find the valley of areolar tissue between the fat associated with the femoral vessels and the fat associated with the bladder. Laterally, the surgeon attempts to scrape off squiggly blood vessels to facilitate separation of the peritoneum from the testicular vessels and vas deferens, which is crucial for indirect inguinal hernia sac takedown.

The surgeon divides the transversalis fascia anteriorly, positioning the vas deferens at approximately the two o'clock position. Using short bursts of energy, the surgeon cauterizes small blood vessels to maintain a bloodless plane of dissection while staying close to the peritoneum.

The surgeon dissects anteriorly, then moves laterally, carefully scraping blood vessels off the peritoneum using bursts of energy for hemostasis. The dissection involves pulling tissue to nine o'clock and scraping to three o'clock to reveal the peritoneal edge. The goal is to avoid picking up the testicular vessels or vas deferens to prevent scrotal testicular pain.

The surgeon carefully peels back the peritoneum to ensure the mesh has good posterior overlap. The dissection is performed in a plane that minimizes vascularity, leaving a shiny fatty layer on the pelvis to protect the lateral femoral cutaneous and genitofemoral nerves. The testicular vessels are visible in this layer, and the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve can be seen.

The surgeon retracts laterally and medially to reveal the areolar plane between the bladder fat and the fat associated with the femoral canal. The dissection progresses towards the obturator space, ensuring the mesh can lay flat between the bladder and the ileum without removing the fatty plug from the obturator space.

The obturator vein is visible, with noticeable edema from pelvic radiation. The robotic, three-dimensional magnified view aids in precise dissection, allowing the surgeon to carefully tease away the pink bladder tissue below the instrument to avoid injury.
The surgeon positions the mesh to tuck into the space of Retzius and transition into the space of Bagros, ensuring that no peritoneum or bladder tissue is caught behind the mesh.

The surgeon demonstrates the transition area of the mesh by pulling medially, revealing the areolar tissue that delineates the plane between the bladder and the fat adjacent to the iliac vein and femoral vessels. The dissection is complete, and the surgeon switches from scissors to a mega suture cut needle driver.

The surgeon identifies fatty tissue potentially indicative of a cord lipoma, using a mega suture cut needle driver to gently tease it off the testicular vessels. Observing its intimate involvement with the vessels, the surgeon decides to leave it in place. A 16x12 cm ProGrip mesh, anatomically designed with a seam and two pieces sewn together, is positioned to ensure excellent lateral and medial coverage. The dissection within the proper plane allows for easy blunt creation of additional space without much energy or use of scissors.

The surgeon places the mesh in a medial to lateral direction, ensuring the seam sits approximately two centimeters below the posterior edge of the internal ring. The mesh is folded for easier unfolding and is tucked between the superior pubic rami, pubis, and bladder. The surgeon checks that no peritoneum is caught between the lower edge of the mesh. The mesh is then unfolded, covering the myelopectineal orifice with good overlap laterally and posteriorly.

The surgeon begins closing the peritoneal flap using a 3-0, 180 V-Lock suture, starting from the lateral side and moving medially. The edges of the peritoneum are closer together laterally, reducing the risk of ripping and tearing during the closure.
The surgeon carefully positions the needle for the next suture, ensuring it is not dropped. They perform three to four suture throws before pulling the suture taut.

Here I'm demonstrating to a case observer surgeon and explaining my technique, which is why you see some pauses. So here I'm showing him what not to do. That's the inefficient way of managing the needle. That's the efficient way of managing the needle.
The surgeon completes the suturing process by making two additional throws, pulling the suture snug, releasing the tension, and cutting the suture flush against the peritoneum.
